title: Epistaxis
oneLiner: Bleeding from the nasal mucosa; common in all ages; can rapidly become life-threatening if posterior or the patient is anticoagulated.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Airway compromise / massive posterior bleed** – The single finding that says “this is the killer”: **gurgling respirations, stridor, or inability to swallow** (blood pooling in pharynx). Act as if the airway is about to obstruct.  
- **Haemorrhagic shock** – The single finding: **SBP <90 or HR >120 despite direct pressure and two attempts at packing**. The posterior bleed can lose >500 mL rapidly; do not underestimate.  
- **Anticoagulant overdose (warfarin/DOAC)** – The single finding: **INR >5 or active bleeding with normal coagulation** – the bleeding is not compressible and reversal is needed now (otherwise exsanguination).  
- **Carotid pseudoaneurysm rupture** (rare but catastrophic) – The single finding: **pulsatile, expanding facial/haematoma** ± active arterial spurting from the nose. This is a surgical emergency, not a nasal packing problem.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  - Bright red blood dripping from one or both nostrils  
  - Blood running down the back of the throat (swallowing, spitting blood)  
  - Sensation of fluid in the mouth/nose, ± gurgling  
  - Dizziness, light-headedness (if significant blood loss)  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - **Anterior bleed:** visible on Anterior Rhinoscopy – Little’s area (Kiesselbach’s plexus) – crisp vessel, often single side.  
  - **Posterior bleed:** blood in oropharynx, no anterior vessel seen, bilateral or profuse.  
  - **Signs of shock:** pallor, tachycardia, hypotension (late).  
  - **Associated findings:** nasal trauma, septal deviation, coagulopathy stigmata (bruising, petechiae).
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely -> less likely, with one-line differentiators**  
  - Anterior epistaxis (Little’s area) – stopped with pressure + visible vessel.  
  - Posterior epistaxis (Woodruff’s plexus) – blood in throat, requires packing.  
  - Trauma/fractured nose – deformity, ecchymosis, history.  
  - Bleeding diathesis (warfarin/DOAC/platelet disorder) – widespread bleeding, easy bruising, abnormal coagulation.  
  - Sinonasal tumour – unilateral, recurrent, ± nasal obstruction, facial pain.  
  - Carotid pseudoaneurysm (rare) – pulsatile mass, expanding haematoma, arterial bleeding.  
- **Splitter:** “Blood dripping from the nose + visible on speculum = anterior bleed” vs. “Blood in oropharynx + no anterior source = posterior bleed”.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**  
  - Vital signs + NEWS2 (monitor for shock)  
  - Anterior rhinoscopy (headlight + speculum) to localise bleed  
  - Assess severity: ask about blood loss, clots, duration  
  - Check for airway patency – listen for stridor, gurgling  
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC (Hb, platelets)  
  - Coagulation screen (INR, APTT) – essential if on anticoagulant/bleeding  
  - Cross-match if massive bleed (e.g., SBP <90, Hb drop)  
  - U&E, LFT if suspect liver disease/coagulopathy  
- **Imaging:**  
  - First-line: CT sinuses if suspected tumour or trauma with septal fracture  
  - Second-line: CT angiography if suspicion of carotid pseudoaneurysm or refractory posterior bleed (uncommon)  
- **Special tests:**  
  - Nasendoscopy by ENT for posterior bleeds or failed packing  
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring:**  
  - No validated emergency score – severity is clinical: anterior vs posterior, shock, ease of control.  
- **Never-miss test:** **Coagulation screen (INR)** – do not start reversal without knowing the level; missing it risks undertreating a major bleed or over-treating a minor one.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - Sit patient upright, lean forward (prevents swallowing/swallowing, reduces venous pressure)  
  - Pinch the cartilaginous part of nose firmly for 10–20 minutes (continuous) – use ice pack on bridge (vasoconstriction)  
  - **If fails:**  
    - Apply topical vasoconstrictor (e.g., co-phenylcaine spray – lidocaine 5% + phenylephrine 0.5%) via spray or soaked cotton pledget – wait 5 min  
    - Chemical cautery (silver nitrate stick) if visible anterior bleed (apply around, not on, the vessel – avoid septal perforation)  
    - Anterior packing: Merocel or Rapid Rhino (inflatable pack) – insert along floor of nasal cavity, inflate with air (20–30 mL), secure with tape; then reassess  
    - **Posterior bleed:** urgent ENT call; place a Foley catheter (12–14 Fr) through the nostril into nasopharynx, inflate balloon (5–10 mL water), pull forward to tamponade – then call for ENT/senior help  
  - **If airway compromise:** call anaesthetics/ICU – consider rapid sequence intubation  
  - **If hypotensive:** IV access (2 × wide bore), crystalloid 500 mL stat, cross-match  
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Keep pack in place for 48–72 hours (anterior) / 24–48 hours (posterior)  
  - Lubricate pack/petroleum jelly to nares  
  - Monitor for rebleeding: observe hourly for 4h, then 4-hourly  
  - Reverse anticoagulation per protocol (consider prothrombin complex concentrate for warfarin, idarucizumab for dabigatran, andexanet alfa for rivaroxaban/apixaban) – follow local policy  
  - Start prophylactic antibiotics if pack left >24h: **co-amoxiclav 625mg PO three times daily** (or doxycycline if penicillin-allergic) to reduce toxic shock syndrome risk  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Nasal moisturisation: saline spray, gel, or emollient (e.g., Naseptin cream if not allergic)  
  - Avoid nose picking, heavy lifting, strenuous activity for 1 week  
  - Treat underlying cause: allergic rhinitis, hypertension (if labile), coagulopathy  
  - If recurrent: consider electrocautery, septoplasty, or arterial ligation/sphenopalatine artery embolisation (ENT referral)  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: IV crystalloid (Hartmann’s/NaCl 0.9%) at maintenance if NBM/bleeding – rate: 100–125 mL/h  
  - VTE prophylaxis: if packing and immobile – low molecular weight heparin only after bleeding stopped and no active coagulopathy – discuss with senior  
  - Diet/NBM: NBM until satisfactory packing and no ongoing swallowing/bleeding – then soft, cool diet  
  - Observation: NEWS2 every 15 min initially, then hourly for 4h, then 4-hourly while pack in situ – escalate if NEWS ≥5
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**  
  - **Topical vasoconstrictor:** co-phenylcaine spray (lidocaine 5% + phenylephrine 0.5%) – one spray into each nostril; can be repeated once after 5 min.  
  - **Silver nitrate cautery:** 75% stick – apply to bleeding point for 2–3 seconds, avoid excess (risk of septal perforation)  
  - **Anterior pack:** Rapid Rhino pack – inflate with air to 20–30 mL (per specific pack size)  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - **Tranexamic acid (topical):** 5 mL of 500 mg/5 mL solution applied to bleeding site on gauze – may reduce recurrence (evidence limited)  
  - **Oral tranexamic acid:** not first-line; can be considered if recurrent epistaxis in hereditary haemorrhagic telangiectasia  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - Silver nitrate: do not use bilateral at same time (risk of septal perforation)  
  - Cocaine: avoid in patients with coronary artery disease, hypertension, or seizure history – use co-phenylcaine instead  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Co-amoxiclav: reduce dose if eGFR <30 (dose interval every 12 hours)  
  - Tranexamic acid: reduce dose in renal impairment (eGFR <30: 500 mg BD max)  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Cocaine (topical)** – prescribing a concentration >5% or multiple doses can cause cardiac ischaemia; **never use in elderly with hypertension** – default to co-phenylcaine.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Airway obstruction (from pack or clot)  
  - Hypovolaemic shock requiring transfusion  
  - Aspiration of blood (pneumonia)  
  - Epistaxis rebleed after pack removal  
  - Toxic shock syndrome (if pack left >48h without antibiotics)  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Septal perforation (from cautery, trauma, or chronic cocaine use)  
  - Adhesions/synechiae (from nasal packs)  
  - Recurrent epistaxis (untreated underlying cause)  
  - Infection (sinusitis, septal abscess)
sections.Prognosis:
- 80–90% resolve with simple first aid; anterior bleeds rarely need admission.  
- Posterior bleeds have ~10% recurrence and higher morbidity (hospital stay, transfusion, ICU).  
- Mortality is <1% but significant in elderly/anticoagulated patients with delayed treatment.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always ask about anticoagulant/antiplatelet use** and check INR before packing (prevents undertreating coagulopathy).  
- **Use topical vasoconstrictor + local anaesthetic** before any nasal instrumentation – improves success, reduces pain and rebleeding.  
- **Consider prophylactic antibiotics if pack left in >24 hours**: co-amoxiclav 625mg TDS (or doxycycline 100mg BD if allergy) – reduces toxic shock risk.  
- **Keep the patient leaning forward** – prevents swallowing blood and reduces venous pressure.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not lay the patient flat** – risk of aspiration and increased venous pressure worsens bleeding.  
- **Do not remove the pack prematurely** – anterior packs need 48–72h; removing earlier increases rebleed.  
- **Do not use silver nitrate on both sides of the septum simultaneously** – high risk of septal perforation.  
- **Do not underestimate blood loss** – posterior bleeds can lose >1 L; a normal Hb on admission may be misleading (haemoconcentration) – repeat FBC.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - Bleeding continues despite 20 minutes of firm pressure (even after pack removal)  
  - Blood in mouth/stool (swallowed blood) or black tarry stools (melaena)  
  - Severe pain, fever, or foul-smelling discharge from nose (infection/toxic shock)  
  - Difficulty breathing or swelling of face/neck  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Return to ENT clinic in 2–4 weeks if pack was placed (pack removal often done in OPD)  
  - If no pack: see GP in 1 week for cautery or further management if recurrent
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (Registrar/Consultant) if:**  
  - Persistent bleeding after two packing attempts  
  - Posterior bleed (blood in throat) requiring Foley catheter  
  - Known coagulopathy with active bleeding (INR >3.5 on warfarin, or on DOAC)  
  - Haemodynamic instability (SBP <90, HR >120) despite fluid resuscitation  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (or any single parameter >3)  
  - GCS <15 (due to blood aspiration or hypoxia)  
- **Call ICU/Crash Team if:**  
  - Airway compromise (stridor, gurgling, inability to maintain airway)  
  - Massive haemorrhage requiring massive transfusion protocol  
  - Refractory hypotension after 2L crystalloid (need for vasopressors)  
  - Cardiac arrest from hypovolaemia or aspiration

rawMarkdown:
## Epistaxis
**One-liner:** Bleeding from the nasal mucosa; common in all ages; can rapidly become life-threatening if posterior or the patient is anticoagulated.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Airway compromise / massive posterior bleed** – The single finding that says “this is the killer”: **gurgling respirations, stridor, or inability to swallow** (blood pooling in pharynx). Act as if the airway is about to obstruct.  
- **Haemorrhagic shock** – The single finding: **SBP <90 or HR >120 despite direct pressure and two attempts at packing**. The posterior bleed can lose >500 mL rapidly; do not underestimate.  
- **Anticoagulant overdose (warfarin/DOAC)** – The single finding: **INR >5 or active bleeding with normal coagulation** – the bleeding is not compressible and reversal is needed now (otherwise exsanguination).  
- **Carotid pseudoaneurysm rupture** (rare but catastrophic) – The single finding: **pulsatile, expanding facial/haematoma** ± active arterial spurting from the nose. This is a surgical emergency, not a nasal packing problem.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  - Bright red blood dripping from one or both nostrils  
  - Blood running down the back of the throat (swallowing, spitting blood)  
  - Sensation of fluid in the mouth/nose, ± gurgling  
  - Dizziness, light-headedness (if significant blood loss)  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - **Anterior bleed:** visible on Anterior Rhinoscopy – Little’s area (Kiesselbach’s plexus) – crisp vessel, often single side.  
  - **Posterior bleed:** blood in oropharynx, no anterior vessel seen, bilateral or profuse.  
  - **Signs of shock:** pallor, tachycardia, hypotension (late).  
  - **Associated findings:** nasal trauma, septal deviation, coagulopathy stigmata (bruising, petechiae).

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely -> less likely, with one-line differentiators**  
  - Anterior epistaxis (Little’s area) – stopped with pressure + visible vessel.  
  - Posterior epistaxis (Woodruff’s plexus) – blood in throat, requires packing.  
  - Trauma/fractured nose – deformity, ecchymosis, history.  
  - Bleeding diathesis (warfarin/DOAC/platelet disorder) – widespread bleeding, easy bruising, abnormal coagulation.  
  - Sinonasal tumour – unilateral, recurrent, ± nasal obstruction, facial pain.  
  - Carotid pseudoaneurysm (rare) – pulsatile mass, expanding haematoma, arterial bleeding.  
- **Splitter:** “Blood dripping from the nose + visible on speculum = anterior bleed” vs. “Blood in oropharynx + no anterior source = posterior bleed”.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**  
  - Vital signs + NEWS2 (monitor for shock)  
  - Anterior rhinoscopy (headlight + speculum) to localise bleed  
  - Assess severity: ask about blood loss, clots, duration  
  - Check for airway patency – listen for stridor, gurgling  
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC (Hb, platelets)  
  - Coagulation screen (INR, APTT) – essential if on anticoagulant/bleeding  
  - Cross-match if massive bleed (e.g., SBP <90, Hb drop)  
  - U&E, LFT if suspect liver disease/coagulopathy  
- **Imaging:**  
  - First-line: CT sinuses if suspected tumour or trauma with septal fracture  
  - Second-line: CT angiography if suspicion of carotid pseudoaneurysm or refractory posterior bleed (uncommon)  
- **Special tests:**  
  - Nasendoscopy by ENT for posterior bleeds or failed packing  
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring:**  
  - No validated emergency score – severity is clinical: anterior vs posterior, shock, ease of control.  
- **Never-miss test:** **Coagulation screen (INR)** – do not start reversal without knowing the level; missing it risks undertreating a major bleed or over-treating a minor one.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - Sit patient upright, lean forward (prevents swallowing/swallowing, reduces venous pressure)  
  - Pinch the cartilaginous part of nose firmly for 10–20 minutes (continuous) – use ice pack on bridge (vasoconstriction)  
  - **If fails:**  
    - Apply topical vasoconstrictor (e.g., co-phenylcaine spray – lidocaine 5% + phenylephrine 0.5%) via spray or soaked cotton pledget – wait 5 min  
    - Chemical cautery (silver nitrate stick) if visible anterior bleed (apply around, not on, the vessel – avoid septal perforation)  
    - Anterior packing: Merocel or Rapid Rhino (inflatable pack) – insert along floor of nasal cavity, inflate with air (20–30 mL), secure with tape; then reassess  
    - **Posterior bleed:** urgent ENT call; place a Foley catheter (12–14 Fr) through the nostril into nasopharynx, inflate balloon (5–10 mL water), pull forward to tamponade – then call for ENT/senior help  
  - **If airway compromise:** call anaesthetics/ICU – consider rapid sequence intubation  
  - **If hypotensive:** IV access (2 × wide bore), crystalloid 500 mL stat, cross-match  
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Keep pack in place for 48–72 hours (anterior) / 24–48 hours (posterior)  
  - Lubricate pack/petroleum jelly to nares  
  - Monitor for rebleeding: observe hourly for 4h, then 4-hourly  
  - Reverse anticoagulation per protocol (consider prothrombin complex concentrate for warfarin, idarucizumab for dabigatran, andexanet alfa for rivaroxaban/apixaban) – follow local policy  
  - Start prophylactic antibiotics if pack left >24h: **co-amoxiclav 625mg PO three times daily** (or doxycycline if penicillin-allergic) to reduce toxic shock syndrome risk  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Nasal moisturisation: saline spray, gel, or emollient (e.g., Naseptin cream if not allergic)  
  - Avoid nose picking, heavy lifting, strenuous activity for 1 week  
  - Treat underlying cause: allergic rhinitis, hypertension (if labile), coagulopathy  
  - If recurrent: consider electrocautery, septoplasty, or arterial ligation/sphenopalatine artery embolisation (ENT referral)  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: IV crystalloid (Hartmann’s/NaCl 0.9%) at maintenance if NBM/bleeding – rate: 100–125 mL/h  
  - VTE prophylaxis: if packing and immobile – low molecular weight heparin only after bleeding stopped and no active coagulopathy – discuss with senior  
  - Diet/NBM: NBM until satisfactory packing and no ongoing swallowing/bleeding – then soft, cool diet  
  - Observation: NEWS2 every 15 min initially, then hourly for 4h, then 4-hourly while pack in situ – escalate if NEWS ≥5

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**  
  - **Topical vasoconstrictor:** co-phenylcaine spray (lidocaine 5% + phenylephrine 0.5%) – one spray into each nostril; can be repeated once after 5 min.  
  - **Silver nitrate cautery:** 75% stick – apply to bleeding point for 2–3 seconds, avoid excess (risk of septal perforation)  
  - **Anterior pack:** Rapid Rhino pack – inflate with air to 20–30 mL (per specific pack size)  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - **Tranexamic acid (topical):** 5 mL of 500 mg/5 mL solution applied to bleeding site on gauze – may reduce recurrence (evidence limited)  
  - **Oral tranexamic acid:** not first-line; can be considered if recurrent epistaxis in hereditary haemorrhagic telangiectasia  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - Silver nitrate: do not use bilateral at same time (risk of septal perforation)  
  - Cocaine: avoid in patients with coronary artery disease, hypertension, or seizure history – use co-phenylcaine instead  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Co-amoxiclav: reduce dose if eGFR <30 (dose interval every 12 hours)  
  - Tranexamic acid: reduce dose in renal impairment (eGFR <30: 500 mg BD max)  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Cocaine (topical)** – prescribing a concentration >5% or multiple doses can cause cardiac ischaemia; **never use in elderly with hypertension** – default to co-phenylcaine.

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Airway obstruction (from pack or clot)  
  - Hypovolaemic shock requiring transfusion  
  - Aspiration of blood (pneumonia)  
  - Epistaxis rebleed after pack removal  
  - Toxic shock syndrome (if pack left >48h without antibiotics)  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Septal perforation (from cautery, trauma, or chronic cocaine use)  
  - Adhesions/synechiae (from nasal packs)  
  - Recurrent epistaxis (untreated underlying cause)  
  - Infection (sinusitis, septal abscess)

### Prognosis
- 80–90% resolve with simple first aid; anterior bleeds rarely need admission.  
- Posterior bleeds have ~10% recurrence and higher morbidity (hospital stay, transfusion, ICU).  
- Mortality is <1% but significant in elderly/anticoagulated patients with delayed treatment.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always ask about anticoagulant/antiplatelet use** and check INR before packing (prevents undertreating coagulopathy).  
- **Use topical vasoconstrictor + local anaesthetic** before any nasal instrumentation – improves success, reduces pain and rebleeding.  
- **Consider prophylactic antibiotics if pack left in >24 hours**: co-amoxiclav 625mg TDS (or doxycycline 100mg BD if allergy) – reduces toxic shock risk.  
- **Keep the patient leaning forward** – prevents swallowing blood and reduces venous pressure.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not lay the patient flat** – risk of aspiration and increased venous pressure worsens bleeding.  
- **Do not remove the pack prematurely** – anterior packs need 48–72h; removing earlier increases rebleed.  
- **Do not use silver nitrate on both sides of the septum simultaneously** – high risk of septal perforation.  
- **Do not underestimate blood loss** – posterior bleeds can lose >1 L; a normal Hb on admission may be misleading (haemoconcentration) – repeat FBC.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  - Bleeding continues despite 20 minutes of firm pressure (even after pack removal)  
  - Blood in mouth/stool (swallowed blood) or black tarry stools (melaena)  
  - Severe pain, fever, or foul-smelling discharge from nose (infection/toxic shock)  
  - Difficulty breathing or swelling of face/neck  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Return to ENT clinic in 2–4 weeks if pack was placed (pack removal often done in OPD)  
  - If no pack: see GP in 1 week for cautery or further management if recurrent

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (Registrar/Consultant) if:**  
  - Persistent bleeding after two packing attempts  
  - Posterior bleed (blood in throat) requiring Foley catheter  
  - Known coagulopathy with active bleeding (INR >3.5 on warfarin, or on DOAC)  
  - Haemodynamic instability (SBP <90, HR >120) despite fluid resuscitation  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (or any single parameter >3)  
  - GCS <15 (due to blood aspiration or hypoxia)  
- **Call ICU/Crash Team if:**  
  - Airway compromise (stridor, gurgling, inability to maintain airway)  
  - Massive haemorrhage requiring massive transfusion protocol  
  - Refractory hypotension after 2L crystalloid (need for vasopressors)  
  - Cardiac arrest from hypovolaemia or aspiration
